author: @Buzzhumma
text: That pressure can simply be created by gravity . Have a stream fill up a tall 2meter high container and at the bottom have a hose . A lot of pressure can be created if you have the fall of the land in your favour . If you have a river or creek that winds back on itself then you could simply have a shorter hose to fill up . Another way is to bury a 2 m long 100 mm pvc pipe upright in the ground with top level with creek . The bottom has a hose that will have pressure at the bottom so this  hose  can be taken elsewhere that 2 m lower than initial creek height !

author: @davemould4638
text: Erm - if you did what I think you did, your measurements are completely wrong.  Did you first put a voltmeter across the open circuit output, then put an ammeter directly across that same output at a later time?  If so you are incorrect.  The current reading is short-circuit current (i.e. close to 0 volts) while the voltage reading is open-circuit voltage.  You have to measure current and voltage *at the same time* while feeding into a suitable load (resistance).   Choose a load that gives half the open circuit voltage to get the max power available.

author: @spaceminions
text: 0.4 watts / 1.7 watts = 23% energy out vs energy in if that was 30psi & 0.5kg/minute by calculating the equivalent head and using gravitational potential energy of m*g*h. Neat for what it's made of. In the limit of low flow high pressure scenarios you begin to be able to use hydraulic motors (ones made for use with water) instead of turbines though.

author: @jima3252
text: Hey Rob, a measurement method I like to use is to use a 10 ohm resistor as a test load. Then just measure voltage across it, divide by 10 to get amps. Then square amps and multiply by 10 to get power. It is more accurate with dc, since you don't need to consider phase angle. Cheers.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice tip mate - cheers

replies.author: @davemould4638
replies.text: To find the maximum power achievable you need to adjust the load to suit the generator/turbine combination.  As the load changes, the output voltage also changes due both to the internal impedance of the generator and the amount that it slows down due to the higher electro-mechanical drag as load current increases.  There is only one point at which the power transfer is at maximum - power will reduce with loads both above and below that point  (Assuming that the max power transfer point is less than that which will cause the generator to overheat).  An MPPT charger is designed to automatically vary its input load so that it is always at the max power transfer point of the supplied input device.  Which can cause some input devices (e.g. an engine-driven alternator) to burn out, so use with caution.

replies.author: @edc1569
replies.text: Indeed, power is volts * amps, but you need to measure them together!

replies.author: @jima3252
replies.text: @davemould4638 good point.

replies.author: @jima3252
replies.text: @edc1569 Please refer to power formula. Amps squared times resistance,  or voltage squared divided by resistance.

author: @prodiver7
text: Your terminology is iffy... When you constrict flow through an orifice you do not increase the pressure of the emerging fluid, you decrease it, but you increase its velocity and kinetic energy. But you still need pressure behind the orifice. This can be produced by the potential energy of a head of water. Otherwise you can use a stream with unconstricted low velocity mass flow to turn a wheel due to gravity.
